## Starting PHASE 0: Deep Codebase Investigation

entries.detail:
1→# NewlandScannerManager with Lifecycle Management
     2→
     3→Implement the NewlandScannerManager as an Application-scoped singleton: register/unregister BroadcastReceiver for nlscan.action.SCANNER_RESULT, handle hardware button (KeyEvent.KEYCODE_F6), programmatic trigger via nlscan.action.SCANNER_TRIG, debounce(300ms) protection against double-scans, and scanEvents: SharedFlow<ScanResult> for reactive consumption by ViewModels.
     4→
     5→## Rationale
     6→The MT90's built-in barcode scanner is the primary input device — not the touchscreen. The existing POC registers a BroadcastReceiver but never unregisters it (memory leak). Competitors like Honeywell suffer from scanner software conflicts (pain-2-1) and Zebra's DataWedge requires complex middleware configuration (pain-1-2). Our direct broadcast integration is simpler and more reliable.
     7→
     8→## User Stories
     9→- As a warehouse worker, I want to press the hardware scan button and have the barcode instantly captured so that I can scan items without touching the screen
    10→- As a developer, I want a reactive scanner API (SharedFlow) so that any screen can observe scan results without tight coupling to the scanner lifecycle
    11→
    12→## Acceptance Criteria
    13→- [ ] NewlandScannerManager is singleton in Application scope (Hilt @Singleton)
    14→- [ ] BroadcastReceiver registers in register() and unregisters in unregister()
    15→- [ ] Hardware scan button (F6) triggers barcode scan
    16→- [ ] Programmatic trigger via trigger(timeoutSec) method works
    17→- [ ] scanEvents SharedFlow emits ScanResult(barcode, barcodeType) to collectors
    18→- [ ] 300ms debounce prevents duplicate scan events
    19→- [ ] No memory leak — receiver properly cleaned up on destroy
    20→- [ ] Validated on physical MT90 device (not emulator)
    21→

entries.detail:
1→# Inventory APK
     2→
     3→An Android application for efficient inventory management and tracking.
     4→
     5→## 📋 Overview
     6→
     7→Inventory APK is a mobile inventory management solution designed to help businesses and individuals track, manage, and organize their inventory items efficiently. Built natively for Android, the application provides a streamlined interface for managing stock levels, tracking item movements, and generating reports on the go.
     8→
     9→## 🎯 Purpose
    10→
    11→Traditional inventory management often requires desktop software or complex web applications. Inventory APK brings the power of inventory tracking directly to your mobile device, enabling:
    12→
    13→- **Real-time tracking**: Update inventory status immediately as items move
    14→- **Offline-first operation**: Manage inventory without constant internet connectivity
    15→- **Mobile convenience**: Use your smartphone to scan, update, and view inventory anywhere
    16→- **Simplified workflow**: Intuitive interface designed for quick data entry and retrieval
    17→
    18→## ✨ Key Features
    19→
    20→### Core Functionality (Planned)
    21→- **Item Management**: Add, edit, and delete inventory items with detailed information
    22→- **Barcode Scanning**: Quick item lookup and data entry using device camera
    23→- **Stock Tracking**: Monitor quantity levels with low-stock alerts
    24→- **Category Organization**: Group items by custom categories and tags
    25→- **Search & Filter**: Quickly find items using powerful search capabilities
    26→- **Offline Support**: Full functionality without internet connection
    27→- **Data Export**: Export inventory data to CSV/Excel formats
    28→
    29→### Advanced Features (Future Roadmap)
    30→- **Multi-location Support**: Manage inventory across multiple warehouses or stores
    31→- **Supplier Management**: Track vendor information and purchase orders
    32→- **Stock Movement History**: Complete audit trail of inventory changes
    33→- **Reporting & Analytics**: Visual insights into inventory trends
    34→- **Cloud Sync**: Synchronize data across multiple devices
    35→- **Multi-user Access**: Collaborative inventory management with role-based permissions
    36→
    37→> **Note**: This project is in active development. See [ROADMAP.md](ROADMAP.md) for detailed feature timeline and MVP scope.
    38→
    39→## 🚀 Getting Started
    40→
    41→### Prerequisites
    42→
    43→Before you begin, ensure you have the following installed:
    44→
    45→- **Android Studio**: Arctic Fox (2020.3.1) or later
    46→- **JDK**: Java Development Kit 11 or higher
    47→- **Android SDK**: API Level 24 (Android 7.0) minimum
    48→- **Git**: For version control
    49→
    50→### Installation
    51→
    52→1. **Clone the repository**
    53→   ```bash
    54→   git clone https://github.com/yourusername/inventory-apk.git
    55→   cd inventory-apk
    56→   ```
    57→
    58→2. **Open in Android Studio**
    59→   - Launch Android Studio
    60→   - Select "Open an Existing Project"
    61→   - Navigate to the cloned repository folder
    62→   - Click "OK"
    63→
    64→3. **Sync Gradle dependencies**
    65→   - Android Studio will automatically prompt to sync
    66→   - Or manually sync via: File → Sync Project with Gradle Files
    67→
    68→4. **Configure Android Emulator** (optional)
    69→   - Open AVD Manager (Tools → AVD Manager)
    70→   - Create a new Virtual Device with API Level 24+
    71→   - Or connect a physical Android device with USB debugging enabled
    72→
    73→5. **Build and Run**
    74→   - Click the "Run" button (green play icon)
    75→   - Select your target device (emulator or physical device)
    76→   - The app will build and install automatically
    77→
    78→### Quick Start Guide
    79→
    80→Once the app is installed:
    81→
    82→1. **First Launch**: The app will create a local database for your inventory
    83→2. **Add Your First Item**:
    84→   - Tap the "+" floating action button
    85→   - Enter item details (name, SKU, quantity, etc.)
    86→   - Optionally scan a barcode for quick entry
    87→   - Save the item
    88→3. **View Inventory**: Browse your items in the main list view
    89→4. **Update Stock**: Tap any item to adjust quantity or edit details
    90→5. **Search Items**: Use the search bar to filter by name, SKU, or category
    91→
    92→> **Note**: As this is a greenfield project, the actual implementation is in progress. Check the [development roadmap](ROADMAP.md) for current status.
    93→
    94→## 📚 Documentation
    95→
    96→- **[ROADMAP.md](ROADMAP.md)** - Development roadmap, MVP scope, and milestones
    97→- **[CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md)** - Guidelines for contributing to the project
    98→- **[docs/ARCHITECTURE.md](docs/ARCHITECTURE.md)** - Technical architecture and design decisions
    99→- **[docs/features/](docs/features/)** - Detailed feature specifications
   100→
   101→## 🏗️ Project Status
   102→
   103→**Current Phase**: Initial Planning & Documentation
   104→
   105→This is a greenfield project currently in the planning and specification phase. Core documentation is being established to guide development. Active development of features will begin once the MVP scope is finalized.
   106→
   107→See [ROADMAP.md](ROADMAP.md) for detailed development timeline and priorities.
   108→
   109→## 🛠️ Technology Stack
   110→
   111→**Target Platform**: Android 7.0 (API 24) and above
   112→
   113→**Planned Technologies**:
   114→- **Language**: Kotlin
   115→- **Architecture**: MVVM (Model-View-ViewModel)
   116→- **Database**: Room (SQLite)
   117→- **Dependency Injection**: Hilt/Dagger
   118→- **Barcode Scanning**: ML Kit or ZXing
   119→- **UI Framework**: Jetpack Compose / Material Design 3
   120→
   121→> See [docs/ARCHITECTURE.md](docs/ARCHITECTURE.md) for detailed technical decisions and rationale.
   122→
   123→## 🤝 Contributing
   124→
   125→We welcome contributions from the community! Whether you're fixing bugs, adding features, or improving documentation, your help is appreciated.
   126→
   127→Please read [CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md) for:
   128→- Development setup instructions
   129→- Coding standards and conventions
   130→- Pull request process
   131→- Issue reporting guidelines
   132→
   133→## 📝 License
   134→
   135→This project's license is to be determined. Check back soon for licensing information.
   136→
   137→## 📧 Contact & Support
   138→
   139→- **Issues**: [GitHub Issues](https://github.com/yourusername/inventory-apk/issues)
   140→- **Discussions**: [GitHub Discussions](https://github.com/yourusername/inventory-apk/discussions)
   141→
   142→## 🙏 Acknowledgments
   143→
   144→This project is built with modern Android development best practices and leverages the Android Jetpack suite of libraries.
   145→
   146→---
   147→
   148→**Status**: 🚧 In Development | **Version**: 0.1.0-alpha | **Last Updated**: March 2026
   149→

1→{
     2→  "feature": "NewlandScannerManager with Lifecycle Management",
     3→  "description": "# NewlandScannerManager with Lifecycle Management

Implement the NewlandScannerManager as an Application-scoped singleton: register/unregister BroadcastReceiver for nlscan.action.SCANNER_RESULT, handle hardware button (KeyEvent.KEYCODE_F6), programmatic trigger via nlscan.action.SCANNER_TRIG, debounce(300ms) protection against double-scans, and scanEvents: SharedFlow<ScanResult> for reactive consumption by ViewModels.

## Rationale
The MT90's built-in barcode scanner is the primary input device — not the touchscreen. The existing POC registers a BroadcastReceiver but never unregisters it (memory leak). Competitors like Honeywell suffer from scanner software conflicts (pain-2-1) and Zebra's DataWedge requires complex middleware configuration (pain-1-2). Our direct broadcast integration is simpler and more reliable.

## User Stories
- As a warehouse worker, I want to press the hardware scan button and have the barcode instantly captured so that I can scan items without touching the screen
- As a developer, I want a reactive scanner API (SharedFlow) so that any screen can observe scan results without tight coupling to the scanner lifecycle

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] NewlandScannerManager is singleton in Application scope (Hilt @Singleton)
- [ ] BroadcastReceiver registers in register() and unregisters in unregister()
- [ ] Hardware scan button (F6) triggers barcode scan
- [ ] Programmatic trigger via trigger(timeoutSec) method works
- [ ] scanEvents SharedFlow emits ScanResult(barcode, barcodeType) to collectors
- [ ] 300ms debounce prevents duplicate scan events
- [ ] No memory leak — receiver properly cleaned up on destroy
- [ ] Validated on physical MT90 device (not emulator)

entries.content:
Perfect! All planning files have been created. Let me create a final summary of the planning session:

## ✅ PLANNING SESSION COMPLETE

I have successfully completed the planning phase for the NewlandScannerManager feature. Here's what was created:

### 📋 Files Created

1. **project_index.json** (1KB)
   - Defined Android app structure
   - Technology stack: Kotlin, Hilt, MVVM, Jetpack Compose
   - Scanner integration details for Newland MT90

2. **context.json** (2.3KB)
   - Documented greenfield status (no existing code)
   - Listed files to create
   - Defined patterns to follow
   - Technical notes about MT90 scanner integration

3. **implementation_plan.json** (17KB)
   - 6 phases with 15 subtasks total
   - Complete verification strategy (medium risk)
   - QA acceptance criteria
   - Parallelism analysis

4. **init.sh** (3.2KB, executable)
   - Environment setup script
   - Prerequisites checker
   - Development instructions

5. **build-progress.txt** (4.4KB)
   - Session log
   - Phase summary
   - Startup command
   - Development notes

### 📊 Implementation Plan Overview

**Workflow Type:** FEATURE

**Phases:**
1. **Android Project Setup** (3 subtasks) - Create Gradle, Hilt, AndroidManifest
2. **Scanner Data Models** (1 subtask) - Create ScanResult data class
3. **NewlandScannerManager Implementation** (7 subtasks) - Core manager with BroadcastReceiver, lifecycle, debouncing, SharedFlow
4. **Dependency Injection Setup** (1 subtask) - Hilt module
5. **Unit Testing** (3 subtasks) - Test debounce, lifecycle, SharedFlow
6. **Device Integration Verification** (2 subtasks) - Sample Activity + MT90 device testing

**Total Subtasks:** 15  
**Recommended Workers:** 1 (sequential execution required)  
**Risk Level:** Medium

### 🎯 Key Technical Requirements

✓ Application-scoped singleton (@Singleton)  
✓ BroadcastReceiver for `nlscan.action.SCANNER_RESULT`  
✓ Hardware scan button (F6) support  
✓ Programmatic trigger via `nlscan.action.SCANNER_TRIG`  
✓ 300ms debounce protection  
✓ `SharedFlow<ScanResult>` for reactive consumption  
✓ Proper lifecycle management (register/unregister)  
✓ No memory leaks

### 🚀 Next Steps

To start implementation, run:

```bash
cd C:\Users\user\Documents\proj\inventory-apk
source auto-claude/.venv/bin/activate
python auto-claude/run.py --spec 019 --parallel 1
```

The coder agent will:
1. Create the Android project structure (Gradle, Hilt setup)
2. Implement the NewlandScannerManager with all required features
3. Create unit tests
4. Build a sample Activity for device testing

### 📝 Important Notes

- **Greenfield Project:** No Android code exists yet - Phase 1 creates entire project structure
- **Device Testing Required:** Physical Newland MT90 needed for final verification (emulator cannot simulate hardware scanner)
- **No Commits Yet:** Planning files are gitignored and managed locally by orchestrator

**Planning session complete. Ready for coder agent to begin implementation!** 🎉
I'll help you implement subtask-1-1 to create the root Gradle build configuration. Let me start by understanding the context and current state.

entries.detail:
1→# NewlandScannerManager with Lifecycle Management
     2→
     3→Implement the NewlandScannerManager as an Application-scoped singleton: register/unregister BroadcastReceiver for nlscan.action.SCANNER_RESULT, handle hardware button (KeyEvent.KEYCODE_F6), programmatic trigger via nlscan.action.SCANNER_TRIG, debounce(300ms) protection against double-scans, and scanEvents: SharedFlow<ScanResult> for reactive consumption by ViewModels.
     4→
     5→## Rationale
     6→The MT90's built-in barcode scanner is the primary input device — not the touchscreen. The existing POC registers a BroadcastReceiver but never unregisters it (memory leak). Competitors like Honeywell suffer from scanner software conflicts (pain-2-1) and Zebra's DataWedge requires complex middleware configuration (pain-1-2). Our direct broadcast integration is simpler and more reliable.
     7→
     8→## User Stories
     9→- As a warehouse worker, I want to press the hardware scan button and have the barcode instantly captured so that I can scan items without touching the screen
    10→- As a developer, I want a reactive scanner API (SharedFlow) so that any screen can observe scan results without tight coupling to the scanner lifecycle
    11→
    12→## Acceptance Criteria
    13→- [ ] NewlandScannerManager is singleton in Application scope (Hilt @Singleton)
    14→- [ ] BroadcastReceiver registers in register() and unregisters in unregister()
    15→- [ ] Hardware scan button (F6) triggers barcode scan
    16→- [ ] Programmatic trigger via trigger(timeoutSec) method works
    17→- [ ] scanEvents SharedFlow emits ScanResult(barcode, barcodeType) to collectors
    18→- [ ] 300ms debounce prevents duplicate scan events
    19→- [ ] No memory leak — receiver properly cleaned up on destroy
    20→- [ ] Validated on physical MT90 device (not emulator)
    21→

=== AUTO-BUILD PROGRESS ===

Project: NewlandScannerManager with Lifecycle Management
Task ID: 019
Started: 2026-03-26

Workflow Type: feature
Rationale: Implementing a new scanner manager component for Newland MT90 barcode scanner integration. This is a greenfield Android feature requiring project setup, core manager implementation with BroadcastReceiver lifecycle management, debouncing, SharedFlow reactive API, and device-specific testing.

Project Status: GREENFIELD
- No Android project structure exists yet
- First task will create basic Android project with Hilt
- Target device: Newland MT90 (Android 7.0+ / API 24+)

Session 1 (Planner):
- Created implementation_plan.json
- Created project_index.json
- Created context.json
- Created init.sh
- Phases: 6
- Total subtasks: 15

Phase Summary:
- Phase 1 (Android Project Setup): 3 subtasks
  - Create Gradle build configuration
  - Set up app module with Hilt dependencies
  - Create AndroidManifest and Application class
  - Depends on: none

- Phase 2 (Scanner Data Models): 1 subtask
  - Create ScanResult data class
  - Depends on: phase-1-project-setup

- Phase 3 (NewlandScannerManager Implementation): 7 subtasks
  - Create manager skeleton with @Singleton
  - Implement BroadcastReceiver for nlscan.action.SCANNER_RESULT
  - Add register()/unregister() lifecycle methods
  - Implement SharedFlow<ScanResult> for reactive events
  - Add 300ms debounce protection
  - Handle hardware button (KEYCODE_F6)
  - Implement programmatic trigger
  - Depends on: phase-2-scanner-models

- Phase 4 (Dependency Injection Setup): 1 subtask
  - Create Hilt ScannerModule
  - Depends on: phase-3-scanner-manager

- Phase 5 (Unit Testing): 3 subtasks
  - Test debounce logic
  - Test register/unregister lifecycle
  - Test SharedFlow event emission
  - Depends on: phase-4-hilt-module

- Phase 6 (Device Integration Verification): 2 subtasks
  - Create sample Activity for testing
  - Manual verification on MT90 device
  - Depends on: phase-5-unit-tests

Services Involved:
- android-app: Single Android application targeting Newland MT90

Technology Stack:
- Language: Kotlin
- Architecture: MVVM
- DI: Hilt
- UI: Jetpack Compose
- Database: Room (SQLite)
- Testing: JUnit4/5, Mockito, Espresso

Key Technical Requirements:
1. Application-scoped singleton (@Singleton)
2. BroadcastReceiver for nlscan.action.SCANNER_RESULT
3. Hardware scan button (F6) support
4. Programmatic trigger via nlscan.action.SCANNER_TRIG
5. 300ms debounce protection
6. SharedFlow<ScanResult> for reactive consumption
7. Proper lifecycle management (register/unregister)
8. No memory leaks

Parallelism Analysis:
- Max parallel phases: 1
- Recommended workers: 1
- Parallel groups: None
- Speedup estimate: Sequential execution required due to strict phase dependencies

Verification Strategy:
- Risk level: medium
- Test types: unit, integration, manual
- Unit tests: Required (./gradlew test)
- Device testing: Required on physical MT90
- Security scanning: Not required
- Staging deployment: Not required

QA Acceptance Criteria:
✓ All unit tests pass
✓ Hardware scan button (F6) works on MT90
✓ Programmatic trigger() method works
✓ 300ms debounce prevents double-scans
✓ No memory leaks - BroadcastReceiver properly unregistered
✓ SharedFlow emits ScanResult events
✓ No crashes or ANRs on device

Example with full path:

  python auto-claude/run.py --spec 019 --parallel 1

Phases 2-6 will implement the scanner manager feature.

- Use "adb logcat | grep Scanner" to monitor scanner events

entries.detail:
1→# NewlandScannerManager with Lifecycle Management
     2→
     3→Implement the NewlandScannerManager as an Application-scoped singleton: register/unregister BroadcastReceiver for nlscan.action.SCANNER_RESULT, handle hardware button (KeyEvent.KEYCODE_F6), programmatic trigger via nlscan.action.SCANNER_TRIG, debounce(300ms) protection against double-scans, and scanEvents: SharedFlow<ScanResult> for reactive consumption by ViewModels.
     4→
     5→## Rationale
     6→The MT90's built-in barcode scanner is the primary input device — not the touchscreen. The existing POC registers a BroadcastReceiver but never unregisters it (memory leak). Competitors like Honeywell suffer from scanner software conflicts (pain-2-1) and Zebra's DataWedge requires complex middleware configuration (pain-1-2). Our direct broadcast integration is simpler and more reliable.
     7→
     8→## User Stories
     9→- As a warehouse worker, I want to press the hardware scan button and have the barcode instantly captured so that I can scan items without touching the screen
    10→- As a developer, I want a reactive scanner API (SharedFlow) so that any screen can observe scan results without tight coupling to the scanner lifecycle
    11→
    12→## Acceptance Criteria
    13→- [ ] NewlandScannerManager is singleton in Application scope (Hilt @Singleton)
    14→- [ ] BroadcastReceiver registers in register() and unregisters in unregister()
    15→- [ ] Hardware scan button (F6) triggers barcode scan
    16→- [ ] Programmatic trigger via trigger(timeoutSec) method works
    17→- [ ] scanEvents SharedFlow emits ScanResult(barcode, barcodeType) to collectors
    18→- [ ] 300ms debounce prevents duplicate scan events
    19→- [ ] No memory leak — receiver properly cleaned up on destroy
    20→- [ ] Validated on physical MT90 device (not emulator)
    21→

But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.

## ✅ Subtask 1-3: Create AndroidManifest and Application class with Hilt - COMPLETED

### Files Created:
1. **app/src/main/AndroidManifest.xml** - Android manifest with:
   - CAMERA and VIBRATE permissions for barcode scanner
   - Application class reference

   - Theme configuration

2. **app/src/main/java/com/inventory/InventoryApplication.kt** - Application class with:
   - `@HiltAndroidApp` annotation for dependency injection
   - onCreate lifecycle hook

3. **Resource files**:
   - strings.xml, themes.xml
   - Launcher icon drawables (adaptive icons)
   - Backup rules and data extraction rules

### Key Challenges Resolved:
1. **KAPT + Java 21 Compatibility Issue**: 
   - Initial build failed with `IllegalAccessError` due to Java module system
   - Resolved by upgrading Kotlin from 1.9.20 to 1.9.22
   - Also required upgrading AGP from 8.2.0 to 8.3.0

2. **Gradle Version Compatibility**:
   - AGP 8.3.0 requires Gradle 8.4
   - Updated gradle-wrapper.properties

3. **Compose Compiler Version**:
   - Kotlin 1.9.22 requires Compose Compiler 1.5.8
   - Updated in app/build.gradle.kts

4. **Kotlin Compilation Strategy**:
   - Set `kotlin.compiler.execution.strategy=in-process` to ensure JVM args inheritance

### Verification:
```bash
./gradlew assembleDebug

entries.detail:
1→# NewlandScannerManager with Lifecycle Management
     2→
     3→Implement the NewlandScannerManager as an Application-scoped singleton: register/unregister BroadcastReceiver for nlscan.action.SCANNER_RESULT, handle hardware button (KeyEvent.KEYCODE_F6), programmatic trigger via nlscan.action.SCANNER_TRIG, debounce(300ms) protection against double-scans, and scanEvents: SharedFlow<ScanResult> for reactive consumption by ViewModels.
     4→
     5→## Rationale
     6→The MT90's built-in barcode scanner is the primary input device — not the touchscreen. The existing POC registers a BroadcastReceiver but never unregisters it (memory leak). Competitors like Honeywell suffer from scanner software conflicts (pain-2-1) and Zebra's DataWedge requires complex middleware configuration (pain-1-2). Our direct broadcast integration is simpler and more reliable.
     7→
     8→## User Stories
     9→- As a warehouse worker, I want to press the hardware scan button and have the barcode instantly captured so that I can scan items without touching the screen
    10→- As a developer, I want a reactive scanner API (SharedFlow) so that any screen can observe scan results without tight coupling to the scanner lifecycle
    11→
    12→## Acceptance Criteria
    13→- [ ] NewlandScannerManager is singleton in Application scope (Hilt @Singleton)
    14→- [ ] BroadcastReceiver registers in register() and unregisters in unregister()
    15→- [ ] Hardware scan button (F6) triggers barcode scan
    16→- [ ] Programmatic trigger via trigger(timeoutSec) method works
    17→- [ ] scanEvents SharedFlow emits ScanResult(barcode, barcodeType) to collectors
    18→- [ ] 300ms debounce prevents duplicate scan events
    19→- [ ] No memory leak — receiver properly cleaned up on destroy
    20→- [ ] Validated on physical MT90 device (not emulator)
    21→

entries.detail:
1→# NewlandScannerManager with Lifecycle Management
     2→
     3→Implement the NewlandScannerManager as an Application-scoped singleton: register/unregister BroadcastReceiver for nlscan.action.SCANNER_RESULT, handle hardware button (KeyEvent.KEYCODE_F6), programmatic trigger via nlscan.action.SCANNER_TRIG, debounce(300ms) protection against double-scans, and scanEvents: SharedFlow<ScanResult> for reactive consumption by ViewModels.
     4→
     5→## Rationale
     6→The MT90's built-in barcode scanner is the primary input device — not the touchscreen. The existing POC registers a BroadcastReceiver but never unregisters it (memory leak). Competitors like Honeywell suffer from scanner software conflicts (pain-2-1) and Zebra's DataWedge requires complex middleware configuration (pain-1-2). Our direct broadcast integration is simpler and more reliable.
     7→
     8→## User Stories
     9→- As a warehouse worker, I want to press the hardware scan button and have the barcode instantly captured so that I can scan items without touching the screen
    10→- As a developer, I want a reactive scanner API (SharedFlow) so that any screen can observe scan results without tight coupling to the scanner lifecycle
    11→
    12→## Acceptance Criteria
    13→- [ ] NewlandScannerManager is singleton in Application scope (Hilt @Singleton)
    14→- [ ] BroadcastReceiver registers in register() and unregisters in unregister()
    15→- [ ] Hardware scan button (F6) triggers barcode scan
    16→- [ ] Programmatic trigger via trigger(timeoutSec) method works
    17→- [ ] scanEvents SharedFlow emits ScanResult(barcode, barcodeType) to collectors
    18→- [ ] 300ms debounce prevents duplicate scan events
    19→- [ ] No memory leak — receiver properly cleaned up on destroy
    20→- [ ] Validated on physical MT90 device (not emulator)
    21→

Excellent! I've successfully completed **subtask-3-2: Implement BroadcastReceiver for nlscan.action.SCANNER_RESULT**.

## What Was Implemented

✅ **BroadcastReceiver Implementation**
- Created an inner `BroadcastReceiver` class that listens for `nlscan.action.SCANNER_RESULT` broadcasts
- Extracts barcode data using `SCAN_BARCODE1` intent extra key
- Extracts barcode type using `SCAN_BARCODE_TYPE` intent extra key
- Validates that barcode data is not empty before processing
- Constructs a `ScanResult` object with the scanned data
- Includes logging for debugging and monitoring

✅ **Code Quality**
- Follows Android BroadcastReceiver best practices
- Uses Newland MT90 SDK patterns for intent extras
- Includes proper null safety and validation
- Added comprehensive KDoc comments
- Follows the existing code style

✅ **Verification**
- ✅ Compilation successful with `./gradlew compileDebugKotlin`
- ✅ Clean commit with descriptive message
- ✅ Subtask status updated in implementation_plan.json

## Next Steps

The BroadcastReceiver is now ready to be registered in the next subtask (**subtask-3-3**), which will implement the `register()` and `unregister()` lifecycle methods. This receiver will then be able to capture scan events from the MT90 hardware scanner.
I'll help you implement the register() and unregister() lifecycle methods for the NewlandScannerManager. Let me start by reading the spec and understanding the requirements.

entries.detail:
1→# NewlandScannerManager with Lifecycle Management
     2→
     3→Implement the NewlandScannerManager as an Application-scoped singleton: register/unregister BroadcastReceiver for nlscan.action.SCANNER_RESULT, handle hardware button (KeyEvent.KEYCODE_F6), programmatic trigger via nlscan.action.SCANNER_TRIG, debounce(300ms) protection against double-scans, and scanEvents: SharedFlow<ScanResult> for reactive consumption by ViewModels.
     4→
     5→## Rationale
     6→The MT90's built-in barcode scanner is the primary input device — not the touchscreen. The existing POC registers a BroadcastReceiver but never unregisters it (memory leak). Competitors like Honeywell suffer from scanner software conflicts (pain-2-1) and Zebra's DataWedge requires complex middleware configuration (pain-1-2). Our direct broadcast integration is simpler and more reliable.
     7→
     8→## User Stories
     9→- As a warehouse worker, I want to press the hardware scan button and have the barcode instantly captured so that I can scan items without touching the screen
    10→- As a developer, I want a reactive scanner API (SharedFlow) so that any screen can observe scan results without tight coupling to the scanner lifecycle
    11→
    12→## Acceptance Criteria
    13→- [ ] NewlandScannerManager is singleton in Application scope (Hilt @Singleton)
    14→- [ ] BroadcastReceiver registers in register() and unregisters in unregister()
    15→- [ ] Hardware scan button (F6) triggers barcode scan
    16→- [ ] Programmatic trigger via trigger(timeoutSec) method works
    17→- [ ] scanEvents SharedFlow emits ScanResult(barcode, barcodeType) to collectors
    18→- [ ] 300ms debounce prevents duplicate scan events
    19→- [ ] No memory leak — receiver properly cleaned up on destroy
    20→- [ ] Validated on physical MT90 device (not emulator)
    21→

1→package com.inventory.scanner
     2→
     3→import android.content.BroadcastReceiver
     4→import android.content.Context
     5→import android.content.Intent
     6→import android.util.Log
     7→import dagger.hilt.android.qualifiers.ApplicationContext
     8→import javax.inject.Inject
     9→import javax.inject.Singleton
    10→
    11→/**
    12→ * Singleton manager for the Newland MT90 barcode scanner.
    13→ *
    14→ * Handles lifecycle management of the scanner BroadcastReceiver, processes scan events,
    15→ * implements debouncing to prevent duplicate scans, and provides a reactive SharedFlow
    16→ * for consuming scan results in ViewModels and UI layers.
    17→ *
    18→ * Key features:
    19→ * - Registers/unregisters BroadcastReceiver for nlscan.action.SCANNER_RESULT
    20→ * - Handles hardware scan button (F6 key)
    21→ * - Supports programmatic scan triggering via nlscan.action.SCANNER_TRIG
    22→ * - 300ms debounce protection against double-scans
    23→ * - Reactive API via SharedFlow<ScanResult>
    24→ *
    25→ * @property context Application context injected by Hilt
    26→ */
    27→@Singleton
    28→class NewlandScannerManager @Inject constructor(
    29→    @ApplicationContext private val context: Context
    30→) {
    31→    companion object {
    32→        private const val TAG = "NewlandScannerManager"
    33→
    34→        // Newland MT90 scanner broadcast actions
    35→        private const val ACTION_SCANNER_RESULT = "nlscan.action.SCANNER_RESULT"
    36→
    37→        // Intent extra keys for scan data (based on Newland MT90 SDK)
    38→        private const val EXTRA_BARCODE_DATA = "SCAN_BARCODE1"
    39→        private const val EXTRA_BARCODE_TYPE = "SCAN_BARCODE_TYPE"
    40→    }
    41→
    42→    /**
    43→     * BroadcastReceiver for handling scan results from the Newland MT90 scanner.
    44→     *
    45→     * Listens for nlscan.action.SCANNER_RESULT broadcasts and extracts barcode data
    46→     * and barcode type from the intent extras.
    47→     */
    48→    private val scanReceiver = object : BroadcastReceiver() {
    49→        override fun onReceive(context: Context?, intent: Intent?) {
    50→            if (intent?.action != ACTION_SCANNER_RESULT) {
    51→                return
    52→            }
    53→
    54→            val barcode = intent.getStringExtra(EXTRA_BARCODE_DATA)
    55→            val barcodeType = intent.getStringExtra(EXTRA_BARCODE_TYPE)
    56→
    57→            if (barcode.isNullOrBlank()) {
    58→                Log.w(TAG, "Received scan result with empty barcode")
    59→                return
    60→            }
    61→
    62→            val scanResult = ScanResult(
    63→                barcode = barcode,
    64→                barcodeType = barcodeType ?: "UNKNOWN"
    65→            )
    66→
    67→            Log.d(TAG, "Scan received: ${scanResult.barcode} (${scanResult.barcodeType})")
    68→
    69→            // Debouncing and SharedFlow emission will be added in subsequent subtasks
    70→        }
    71→    }
    72→}
    73→

    58→            }
    59→
    60→            val barcode = intent.getStringExtra(EXTRA_BARCODE_DATA)
    61→            val barcodeType = intent.getStringExtra(EXTRA_BARCODE_TYPE)
    62→
    63→            if (barcode.isNullOrBlank()) {
    64→                Log.w(TAG, "Received scan result with empty barcode")
    65→                return
    66→            }
    67→
    68→            val scanResult = ScanResult(
    69→                barcode = barcode,
    70→                barcodeType = barcodeType ?: "UNKNOWN"
    71→            )
    72→
    73→            Log.d(TAG, "Scan received: ${scanResult.barcode} (${scanResult.barcodeType})")
    74→
    75→            // Debouncing and SharedFlow emission will be added in subsequent subtasks
    76→        }
    77→    }
    78→
    79→    /**
    80→     * Registers the BroadcastReceiver to listen for scanner broadcasts.
    81→     *
    82→     * This should be called when the Activity/Fragment enters the foreground
    83→     * (typically in onResume or onStart) to begin receiving scan events.
    84→     *
    85→     * Safe to call multiple times - will not double-register.
    86→     */
    87→    fun register() {
    88→        if (isRegistered) {
    89→            Log.d(TAG, "Scanner receiver already registered, skipping")
    90→            return
    91→        }
    92→
    93→        val filter = android.content.IntentFilter(ACTION_SCANNER_RESULT)
    94→        context.registerReceiver(scanReceiver, filter)
    95→        isRegistered = true
    96→        Log.d(TAG, "Scanner receiver registered")
    97→    }
    98→
    99→    /**
   100→     * Unregisters the BroadcastReceiver to stop listening for scanner broadcasts.
   101→     *
   102→     * This MUST be called when the Activity/Fragment is destroyed (typically in
   103→     * onPause or onStop) to prevent memory leaks.
   104→     *
   105→     * Safe to call multiple times - will not crash if already unregistered.
   106→     */
   107→    fun unregister() {
   108→        if (!isRegistered) {
   109→            Log.d(TAG, "Scanner receiver not registered, skipping unregister")
   110→            return
   111→        }
   112→
   113→        context.unregisterReceiver(scanReceiver)
   114→        isRegistered = false
   115→        Log.d(TAG, "Scanner receiver unregistered")
   116→    }
   117→}
   118→

## ✅ Subtask Completed Successfully!

I've successfully implemented the `register()` and `unregister()` lifecycle methods for the NewlandScannerManager. Here's what was accomplished:

### Implementation Details:

1. **Registration State Tracking**
   - Added `isRegistered` private flag to prevent double registration/unregistration

2. **register() Method**
   - Creates an IntentFilter for `ACTION_SCANNER_RESULT`
   - Registers the BroadcastReceiver with the application context
   - Safe to call multiple times - checks registration state first
   - Includes logging for debugging

3. **unregister() Method**
   - Safely unregisters the BroadcastReceiver
   - Prevents memory leaks by properly cleaning up
   - Safe to call multiple times - checks registration state first
   - Includes logging for debugging

4. **Documentation**
   - Added comprehensive KDoc comments explaining:
     - When to call each method (onResume/onStart for register, onPause/onStop for unregister)
     - Safety guarantees (no double registration/crashes)
     - Memory leak prevention importance

### Verification:
- ✅ Compilation successful: `./gradlew compileDebugKotlin`
- ✅ Changes committed to git
- ✅ Implementation plan updated

### Quality Checklist:
- ✅ Follows Android lifecycle-aware component patterns
- ✅ No debugging statements (only proper logging)
- ✅ Error handling in place (registration state checks)
- ✅ Verification passes
- ✅ Clean commit with descriptive message

entries.detail:
1→# NewlandScannerManager with Lifecycle Management
     2→
     3→Implement the NewlandScannerManager as an Application-scoped singleton: register/unregister BroadcastReceiver for nlscan.action.SCANNER_RESULT, handle hardware button (KeyEvent.KEYCODE_F6), programmatic trigger via nlscan.action.SCANNER_TRIG, debounce(300ms) protection against double-scans, and scanEvents: SharedFlow<ScanResult> for reactive consumption by ViewModels.
     4→
     5→## Rationale
     6→The MT90's built-in barcode scanner is the primary input device — not the touchscreen. The existing POC registers a BroadcastReceiver but never unregisters it (memory leak). Competitors like Honeywell suffer from scanner software conflicts (pain-2-1) and Zebra's DataWedge requires complex middleware configuration (pain-1-2). Our direct broadcast integration is simpler and more reliable.
     7→
     8→## User Stories
     9→- As a warehouse worker, I want to press the hardware scan button and have the barcode instantly captured so that I can scan items without touching the screen
    10→- As a developer, I want a reactive scanner API (SharedFlow) so that any screen can observe scan results without tight coupling to the scanner lifecycle
    11→
    12→## Acceptance Criteria
    13→- [ ] NewlandScannerManager is singleton in Application scope (Hilt @Singleton)
    14→- [ ] BroadcastReceiver registers in register() and unregisters in unregister()
    15→- [ ] Hardware scan button (F6) triggers barcode scan
    16→- [ ] Programmatic trigger via trigger(timeoutSec) method works
    17→- [ ] scanEvents SharedFlow emits ScanResult(barcode, barcodeType) to collectors
    18→- [ ] 300ms debounce prevents duplicate scan events
    19→- [ ] No memory leak — receiver properly cleaned up on destroy
    20→- [ ] Validated on physical MT90 device (not emulator)
    21→

1→package com.inventory.scanner
     2→
     3→import android.content.BroadcastReceiver
     4→import android.content.Context
     5→import android.content.Intent
     6→import android.util.Log
     7→import dagger.hilt.android.qualifiers.ApplicationContext
     8→import javax.inject.Inject
     9→import javax.inject.Singleton
    10→
    11→/**
    12→ * Singleton manager for the Newland MT90 barcode scanner.
    13→ *
    14→ * Handles lifecycle management of the scanner BroadcastReceiver, processes scan events,
    15→ * implements debouncing to prevent duplicate scans, and provides a reactive SharedFlow
    16→ * for consuming scan results in ViewModels and UI layers.
    17→ *
    18→ * Key features:
    19→ * - Registers/unregisters BroadcastReceiver for nlscan.action.SCANNER_RESULT
    20→ * - Handles hardware scan button (F6 key)
    21→ * - Supports programmatic scan triggering via nlscan.action.SCANNER_TRIG
    22→ * - 300ms debounce protection against double-scans
    23→ * - Reactive API via SharedFlow<ScanResult>
    24→ *
    25→ * @property context Application context injected by Hilt
    26→ */
    27→@Singleton
    28→class NewlandScannerManager @Inject constructor(
    29→    @ApplicationContext private val context: Context
    30→) {
    31→    companion object {
    32→        private const val TAG = "NewlandScannerManager"
    33→
    34→        // Newland MT90 scanner broadcast actions
    35→        private const val ACTION_SCANNER_RESULT = "nlscan.action.SCANNER_RESULT"
    36→
    37→        // Intent extra keys for scan data (based on Newland MT90 SDK)
    38→        private const val EXTRA_BARCODE_DATA = "SCAN_BARCODE1"
    39→        private const val EXTRA_BARCODE_TYPE = "SCAN_BARCODE_TYPE"
    40→    }
    41→
    42→    /**
    43→     * Tracks whether the BroadcastReceiver is currently registered.
    44→     * Prevents double registration/unregistration.
    45→     */
    46→    private var isRegistered = false
    47→
    48→    /**
    49→     * BroadcastReceiver for handling scan results from the Newland MT90 scanner.
    50→     *
    51→     * Listens for nlscan.action.SCANNER_RESULT broadcasts and extracts barcode data
    52→     * and barcode type from the intent extras.
    53→     */
    54→    private val scanReceiver = object : BroadcastReceiver() {
    55→        override fun onReceive(context: Context?, intent: Intent?) {
    56→            if (intent?.action != ACTION_SCANNER_RESULT) {
    57→                return
    58→            }
    59→
    60→            val barcode = intent.getStringExtra(EXTRA_BARCODE_DATA)
    61→            val barcodeType = intent.getStringExtra(EXTRA_BARCODE_TYPE)
    62→
    63→            if (barcode.isNullOrBlank()) {
    64→                Log.w(TAG, "Received scan result with empty barcode")
    65→                return
    66→            }
    67→
    68→            val scanResult = ScanResult(
    69→                barcode = barcode,
    70→                barcodeType = barcodeType ?: "UNKNOWN"
    71→            )
    72→
    73→            Log.d(TAG, "Scan received: ${scanResult.barcode} (${scanResult.barcodeType})")
    74→
    75→            // Debouncing and SharedFlow emission will be added in subsequent subtasks
    76→        }
    77→    }
    78→
    79→    /**
    80→     * Registers the BroadcastReceiver to listen for scanner broadcasts.
    81→     *
    82→     * This should be called when the Activity/Fragment enters the foreground
    83→     * (typically in onResume or onStart) to begin receiving scan events.
    84→     *
    85→     * Safe to call multiple times - will not double-register.
    86→     */
    87→    fun register() {
    88→        if (isRegistered) {
    89→            Log.d(TAG, "Scanner receiver already registered, skipping")
    90→            return
    91→        }
    92→
    93→        val filter = android.content.IntentFilter(ACTION_SCANNER_RESULT)
    94→        context.registerReceiver(scanReceiver, filter)
    95→        isRegistered = true
    96→        Log.d(TAG, "Scanner receiver registered")
    97→    }
    98→
    99→    /**
   100→     * Unregisters the BroadcastReceiver to stop listening for scanner broadcasts.
   101→     *
   102→     * This MUST be called when the Activity/Fragment is destroyed (typically in
   103→     * onPause or onStop) to prevent memory leaks.
   104→     *
   105→     * Safe to call multiple times - will not crash if already unregistered.
   106→     */
   107→    fun unregister() {
   108→        if (!isRegistered) {
   109→            Log.d(TAG, "Scanner receiver not registered, skipping unregister")
   110→            return
   111→        }
   112→
   113→        context.unregisterReceiver(scanReceiver)
   114→        isRegistered = false
   115→        Log.d(TAG, "Scanner receiver unregistered")
   116→    }
   117→}
   118→

1→package com.inventory.scanner
     2→
     3→import android.content.BroadcastReceiver
     4→import android.content.Context
     5→import android.content.Intent
     6→import android.util.Log
     7→import dagger.hilt.android.qualifiers.ApplicationContext
     8→import kotlinx.coroutines.CoroutineScope
     9→import kotlinx.coroutines.Dispatchers
    10→import kotlinx.coroutines.SupervisorJob
    11→import kotlinx.coroutines.flow.MutableSharedFlow
    12→import kotlinx.coroutines.flow.SharedFlow
    13→import kotlinx.coroutines.flow.asSharedFlow
    14→import kotlinx.coroutines.launch
    15→import javax.inject.Inject
    16→import javax.inject.Singleton
    17→
    18→/**
    19→ * Singleton manager for the Newland MT90 barcode scanner.
    20→ *
    21→ * Handles lifecycle management of the scanner BroadcastReceiver, processes scan events,
    22→ * implements debouncing to prevent duplicate scans, and provides a reactive SharedFlow
    23→ * for consuming scan results in ViewModels and UI layers.
    24→ *
    25→ * Key features:
    26→ * - Registers/unregisters BroadcastReceiver for nlscan.action.SCANNER_RESULT
    27→ * - Handles hardware scan button (F6 key)
    28→ * - Supports programmatic scan triggering via nlscan.action.SCANNER_TRIG
    29→ * - 300ms debounce protection against double-scans
    30→ * - Reactive API via SharedFlow<ScanResult>
    31→ *
    32→ * @property context Application context injected by Hilt
    33→ */
    34→@Singleton
    35→class NewlandScannerManager @Inject constructor(
    36→    @ApplicationContext private val context: Context
    37→) {
    38→    companion object {
    39→        private const val TAG = "NewlandScannerManager"
    40→
    41→        // Newland MT90 scanner broadcast actions
    42→        private const val ACTION_SCANNER_RESULT = "nlscan.action.SCANNER_RESULT"
    43→
    44→        // Intent extra keys for scan data (based on Newland MT90 SDK)
    45→        private const val EXTRA_BARCODE_DATA = "SCAN_BARCODE1"
    46→        private const val EXTRA_BARCODE_TYPE = "SCAN_BARCODE_TYPE"
    47→    }
    48→
    49→    /**
    50→     * Tracks whether the BroadcastReceiver is currently registered.
    51→     * Prevents double registration/unregistration.
    52→     */
    53→    private var isRegistered = false
    54→
    55→    /**
    56→     * CoroutineScope for emitting scan events to SharedFlow.
    57→     * Uses SupervisorJob to isolate failures and Dispatchers.Main for UI thread safety.
    58→     */
    59→    private val scannerScope = CoroutineScope(SupervisorJob() + Dispatchers.Main.immediate)
    60→
    61→    /**
    62→     * Internal MutableSharedFlow for emitting scan results.
    63→     * Configured with replay = 0 (no replay) and extraBufferCapacity = 1 to buffer
    64→     * one event if no collectors are actively listening.
    65→     */
    66→    private val _scanEvents = MutableSharedFlow<ScanResult>(
    67→        replay = 0,
    68→        extraBufferCapacity = 1
    69→    )
    70→
    71→    /**
    72→     * Public SharedFlow for consuming scan results in ViewModels and UI layers.
    73→     *
    74→     * Collectors will receive ScanResult events emitted when the scanner captures
    75→     * a barcode. Use this in ViewModels with viewModelScope.launch { scanEvents.collect {...} }
    76→     *
    77→     * Example usage:
    78→     * ```
    79→     * viewModelScope.launch {
    80→     *     scannerManager.scanEvents.collect { scanResult ->
    81→     *         Log.d("MyViewModel", "Scanned: ${scanResult.barcode}")
    82→     *     }
    83→     * }
    84→     * ```
    85→     */
    86→    val scanEvents: SharedFlow<ScanResult> = _scanEvents.asSharedFlow()
    87→
    88→    /**
    89→     * BroadcastReceiver for handling scan results from the Newland MT90 scanner.
    90→     *
    91→     * Listens for nlscan.action.SCANNER_RESULT broadcasts and extracts barcode data
    92→     * and barcode type from the intent extras.
    93→     */
    94→    private val scanReceiver = object : BroadcastReceiver() {
    95→        override fun onReceive(context: Context?, intent: Intent?) {
    96→            if (intent?.action != ACTION_SCANNER_RESULT) {
    97→                return
    98→            }
    99→
   100→            val barcode = intent.getStringExtra(EXTRA_BARCODE_DATA)
   101→            val barcodeType = intent.getStringExtra(EXTRA_BARCODE_TYPE)
   102→
   103→            if (barcode.isNullOrBlank()) {
   104→                Log.w(TAG, "Received scan result with empty barcode")
   105→                return
   106→            }
   107→
   108→            val scanResult = ScanResult(
   109→                barcode = barcode,
   110→                barcodeType = barcodeType ?: "UNKNOWN"
   111→            )
   112→
   113→            Log.d(TAG, "Scan received: ${scanResult.barcode} (${scanResult.barcodeType})")
   114→
   115→            // Emit scan result to SharedFlow for reactive consumption
   116→            // Note: Debouncing will be added in the next subtask
   117→            scannerScope.launch {
   118→                _scanEvents.emit(scanResult)
   119→                Log.d(TAG, "Scan event emitted to SharedFlow")
   120→            }
   121→        }
   122→    }
   123→
   124→    /**
   125→     * Registers the BroadcastReceiver to listen for scanner broadcasts.
   126→     *
   127→     * This should be called when the Activity/Fragment enters the foreground
   128→     * (typically in onResume or onStart) to begin receiving scan events.
   129→     *
   130→     * Safe to call multiple times - will not double-register.
   131→     */
   132→    fun register() {
   133→        if (isRegistered) {
   134→            Log.d(TAG, "Scanner receiver already registered, skipping")
   135→            return
   136→        }
   137→
   138→        val filter = android.content.IntentFilter(ACTION_SCANNER_RESULT)
   139→        context.registerReceiver(scanReceiver, filter)
   140→        isRegistered = true
   141→        Log.d(TAG, "Scanner receiver registered")
   142→    }
   143→
   144→    /**
   145→     * Unregisters the BroadcastReceiver to stop listening for scanner broadcasts.
   146→     *
   147→     * This MUST be called when the Activity/Fragment is destroyed (typically in
   148→     * onPause or onStop) to prevent memory leaks.
   149→     *
   150→     * Safe to call multiple times - will not crash if already unregistered.
   151→     */
   152→    fun unregister() {
   153→        if (!isRegistered) {
   154→            Log.d(TAG, "Scanner receiver not registered, skipping unregister")
   155→            return
   156→        }
   157→
   158→        context.unregisterReceiver(scanReceiver)
   159→        isRegistered = false
   160→        Log.d(TAG, "Scanner receiver unregistered")
   161→    }
   162→}
   163→

Perfect! ✅ **Subtask 3-4 completed successfully!**

## What was implemented:

I've successfully added reactive event emission to the `NewlandScannerManager` using Kotlin SharedFlow:

### Key additions:
1. **CoroutineScope** - Created with `SupervisorJob` and `Dispatchers.Main.immediate` for safe, isolated event emission
2. **MutableSharedFlow** - Internal flow configured with `replay=0` and `extraBufferCapacity=1` to buffer one event
3. **Public SharedFlow** - Exposed via `scanEvents` property for consumption in ViewModels and UI layers
4. **Emission logic** - Updated `BroadcastReceiver` to emit `ScanResult` events to the SharedFlow
5. **Comprehensive documentation** - Added KDoc comments with usage examples

### Example usage (from the KDoc):
```kotlin
viewModelScope.launch {
    scannerManager.scanEvents.collect { scanResult ->
        Log.d("MyViewModel", "Scanned: ${scanResult.barcode}")
    }
}
```

### Verification:
- ✅ Compilation successful (`./gradlew compileDebugKotlin`)
- ✅ Changes committed to git
- ✅ Subtask status updated to completed

The reactive API is now ready for ViewModels to consume scan events. The next subtask will add 300ms debounce protection against double-scans.
I'll implement the 300ms debounce protection for the NewlandScannerManager. Let me start by reading the spec and understanding the pattern.

entries.detail:
1→# NewlandScannerManager with Lifecycle Management
     2→
     3→Implement the NewlandScannerManager as an Application-scoped singleton: register/unregister BroadcastReceiver for nlscan.action.SCANNER_RESULT, handle hardware button (KeyEvent.KEYCODE_F6), programmatic trigger via nlscan.action.SCANNER_TRIG, debounce(300ms) protection against double-scans, and scanEvents: SharedFlow<ScanResult> for reactive consumption by ViewModels.
     4→
     5→## Rationale
     6→The MT90's built-in barcode scanner is the primary input device — not the touchscreen. The existing POC registers a BroadcastReceiver but never unregisters it (memory leak). Competitors like Honeywell suffer from scanner software conflicts (pain-2-1) and Zebra's DataWedge requires complex middleware configuration (pain-1-2). Our direct broadcast integration is simpler and more reliable.
     7→
     8→## User Stories
     9→- As a warehouse worker, I want to press the hardware scan button and have the barcode instantly captured so that I can scan items without touching the screen
    10→- As a developer, I want a reactive scanner API (SharedFlow) so that any screen can observe scan results without tight coupling to the scanner lifecycle
    11→
    12→## Acceptance Criteria
    13→- [ ] NewlandScannerManager is singleton in Application scope (Hilt @Singleton)
    14→- [ ] BroadcastReceiver registers in register() and unregisters in unregister()
    15→- [ ] Hardware scan button (F6) triggers barcode scan
    16→- [ ] Programmatic trigger via trigger(timeoutSec) method works
    17→- [ ] scanEvents SharedFlow emits ScanResult(barcode, barcodeType) to collectors
    18→- [ ] 300ms debounce prevents duplicate scan events
    19→- [ ] No memory leak — receiver properly cleaned up on destroy
    20→- [ ] Validated on physical MT90 device (not emulator)
    21→

1→package com.inventory.scanner
     2→
     3→import android.content.BroadcastReceiver
     4→import android.content.Context
     5→import android.content.Intent
     6→import android.util.Log
     7→import dagger.hilt.android.qualifiers.ApplicationContext
     8→import kotlinx.coroutines.CoroutineScope
     9→import kotlinx.coroutines.Dispatchers
    10→import kotlinx.coroutines.SupervisorJob
    11→import kotlinx.coroutines.flow.MutableSharedFlow
    12→import kotlinx.coroutines.flow.SharedFlow
    13→import kotlinx.coroutines.flow.asSharedFlow
    14→import kotlinx.coroutines.launch
    15→import javax.inject.Inject
    16→import javax.inject.Singleton
    17→
    18→/**
    19→ * Singleton manager for the Newland MT90 barcode scanner.
    20→ *
    21→ * Handles lifecycle management of the scanner BroadcastReceiver, processes scan events,
    22→ * implements debouncing to prevent duplicate scans, and provides a reactive SharedFlow
    23→ * for consuming scan results in ViewModels and UI layers.
    24→ *
    25→ * Key features:
    26→ * - Registers/unregisters BroadcastReceiver for nlscan.action.SCANNER_RESULT
    27→ * - Handles hardware scan button (F6 key)
    28→ * - Supports programmatic scan triggering via nlscan.action.SCANNER_TRIG
    29→ * - 300ms debounce protection against double-scans
    30→ * - Reactive API via SharedFlow<ScanResult>
    31→ *
    32→ * @property context Application context injected by Hilt
    33→ */
    34→@Singleton
    35→class NewlandScannerManager @Inject constructor(
    36→    @ApplicationContext private val context: Context
    37→) {
    38→    companion object {
    39→        private const val TAG = "NewlandScannerManager"
    40→
    41→        // Newland MT90 scanner broadcast actions
    42→        private const val ACTION_SCANNER_RESULT = "nlscan.action.SCANNER_RESULT"
    43→
    44→        // Intent extra keys for scan data (based on Newland MT90 SDK)
    45→        private const val EXTRA_BARCODE_DATA = "SCAN_BARCODE1"
    46→        private const val EXTRA_BARCODE_TYPE = "SCAN_BARCODE_TYPE"
    47→    }
    48→
    49→    /**
    50→     * Tracks whether the BroadcastReceiver is currently registered.
    51→     * Prevents double registration/unregistration.
    52→     */
    53→    private var isRegistered = false
    54→
    55→    /**
    56→     * CoroutineScope for emitting scan events to SharedFlow.
    57→     * Uses SupervisorJob to isolate failures and Dispatchers.Main for UI thread safety.
    58→     */
    59→    private val scannerScope = CoroutineScope(SupervisorJob() + Dispatchers.Main.immediate)
    60→
    61→    /**
    62→     * Internal MutableSharedFlow for emitting scan results.
    63→     * Configured with replay = 0 (no replay) and extraBufferCapacity = 1 to buffer
    64→     * one event if no collectors are actively listening.
    65→     */
    66→    private val _scanEvents = MutableSharedFlow<ScanResult>(
    67→        replay = 0,
    68→        extraBufferCapacity = 1
    69→    )
    70→
    71→    /**
    72→     * Public SharedFlow for consuming scan results in ViewModels and UI layers.
    73→     *
    74→     * Collectors will receive ScanResult events emitted when the scanner captures
    75→     * a barcode. Use this in ViewModels with viewModelScope.launch { scanEvents.collect {...} }
    76→     *
    77→     * Example usage:
    78→     * ```
    79→     * viewModelScope.launch {
    80→     *     scannerManager.scanEvents.collect { scanResult ->
    81→     *         Log.d("MyViewModel", "Scanned: ${scanResult.barcode}")
    82→     *     }
    83→     * }
    84→     * ```
    85→     */
    86→    val scanEvents: SharedFlow<ScanResult> = _scanEvents.asSharedFlow()
    87→
    88→    /**
    89→     * BroadcastReceiver for handling scan results from the Newland MT90 scanner.
    90→     *
    91→     * Listens for nlscan.action.SCANNER_RESULT broadcasts and extracts barcode data
    92→     * and barcode type from the intent extras.
    93→     */
    94→    private val scanReceiver = object : BroadcastReceiver() {
    95→        override fun onReceive(context: Context?, intent: Intent?) {
    96→            if (intent?.action != ACTION_SCANNER_RESULT) {
    97→                return
    98→            }
    99→
   100→            val barcode = intent.getStringExtra(EXTRA_BARCODE_DATA)
   101→            val barcodeType = intent.getStringExtra(EXTRA_BARCODE_TYPE)
   102→
   103→            if (barcode.isNullOrBlank()) {
   104→                Log.w(TAG, "Received scan result with empty barcode")
   105→                return
   106→            }
   107→
   108→            val scanResult = ScanResult(
   109→                barcode = barcode,
   110→                barcodeType = barcodeType ?: "UNKNOWN"
   111→            )
   112→
   113→            Log.d(TAG, "Scan received: ${scanResult.barcode} (${scanResult.barcodeType})")
   114→
   115→            // Emit scan result to SharedFlow for reactive consumption
   116→            // Note: Debouncing will be added in the next subtask
   117→            scannerScope.launch {
   118→                _scanEvents.emit(scanResult)
   119→                Log.d(TAG, "Scan event emitted to SharedFlow")
   120→            }
   121→        }
   122→    }
   123→
   124→    /**
   125→     * Registers the BroadcastReceiver to listen for scanner broadcasts.
   126→     *
   127→     * This should be called when the Activity/Fragment enters the foreground
   128→     * (typically in onResume or onStart) to begin receiving scan events.
   129→     *
   130→     * Safe to call multiple times - will not double-register.
   131→     */
   132→    fun register() {
   133→        if (isRegistered) {
   134→            Log.d(TAG, "Scanner receiver already registered, skipping")
   135→            return
   136→        }
   137→
   138→        val filter = android.content.IntentFilter(ACTION_SCANNER_RESULT)
   139→        context.registerReceiver(scanReceiver, filter)
   140→        isRegistered = true
   141→        Log.d(TAG, "Scanner receiver registered")
   142→    }
   143→
   144→    /**
   145→     * Unregisters the BroadcastReceiver to stop listening for scanner broadcasts.
   146→     *
   147→     * This MUST be called when the Activity/Fragment is destroyed (typically in
   148→     * onPause or onStop) to prevent memory leaks.
   149→     *
   150→     * Safe to call multiple times - will not crash if already unregistered.
   151→     */
   152→    fun unregister() {
   153→        if (!isRegistered) {
   154→            Log.d(TAG, "Scanner receiver not registered, skipping unregister")
   155→            return
   156→        }
   157→
   158→        context.unregisterReceiver(scanReceiver)
   159→        isRegistered = false
   160→        Log.d(TAG, "Scanner receiver unregistered")
   161→    }
   162→}
   163→

entries.content:
## ✅ Subtask Completed Successfully

I've successfully implemented **300ms debounce protection** for the NewlandScannerManager to prevent duplicate scans. Here's what was done:

### Changes Made:

1. **Added debounce constant** (`DEBOUNCE_DELAY_MS = 300L`) to the companion object
2. **Added timestamp tracking** (`lastScanTimestamp = 0L`) to track the last processed scan
3. **Implemented debounce logic** in the `BroadcastReceiver.onReceive()` method:
   - Calculates time elapsed since last scan
   - Ignores scans within 300ms window (logs and returns early)
   - Updates timestamp before emitting to prevent race conditions
   - Emits valid scans to SharedFlow

### Key Features:
- ✅ Timestamp-based debouncing using `System.currentTimeMillis()`
- ✅ Clear debug logging for ignored scans
- ✅ Race condition prevention by updating timestamp before emit
- ✅ Follows Kotlin and Android best practices

### Verification:
- ✅ Compilation successful with `./gradlew compileDebugKotlin`
- ✅ Committed to git with descriptive message
- ✅ Implementation plan updated to "completed" status

The debounce protection will prevent the scanner from processing duplicate scans when users accidentally press the hardware scan button (F6) multiple times in quick succession.
I'll implement the hardware button handler for KEYCODE_F6. Let me start by reading the spec and context to understand the requirements better.

entries.detail:
1→# NewlandScannerManager with Lifecycle Management
     2→
     3→Implement the NewlandScannerManager as an Application-scoped singleton: register/unregister BroadcastReceiver for nlscan.action.SCANNER_RESULT, handle hardware button (KeyEvent.KEYCODE_F6), programmatic trigger via nlscan.action.SCANNER_TRIG, debounce(300ms) protection against double-scans, and scanEvents: SharedFlow<ScanResult> for reactive consumption by ViewModels.
     4→
     5→## Rationale
     6→The MT90's built-in barcode scanner is the primary input device — not the touchscreen. The existing POC registers a BroadcastReceiver but never unregisters it (memory leak). Competitors like Honeywell suffer from scanner software conflicts (pain-2-1) and Zebra's DataWedge requires complex middleware configuration (pain-1-2). Our direct broadcast integration is simpler and more reliable.
     7→
     8→## User Stories
     9→- As a warehouse worker, I want to press the hardware scan button and have the barcode instantly captured so that I can scan items without touching the screen
    10→- As a developer, I want a reactive scanner API (SharedFlow) so that any screen can observe scan results without tight coupling to the scanner lifecycle
    11→
    12→## Acceptance Criteria
    13→- [ ] NewlandScannerManager is singleton in Application scope (Hilt @Singleton)
    14→- [ ] BroadcastReceiver registers in register() and unregisters in unregister()
    15→- [ ] Hardware scan button (F6) triggers barcode scan
    16→- [ ] Programmatic trigger via trigger(timeoutSec) method works
    17→- [ ] scanEvents SharedFlow emits ScanResult(barcode, barcodeType) to collectors
    18→- [ ] 300ms debounce prevents duplicate scan events
    19→- [ ] No memory leak — receiver properly cleaned up on destroy
    20→- [ ] Validated on physical MT90 device (not emulator)
    21→

1→package com.inventory.scanner
     2→
     3→import android.content.BroadcastReceiver
     4→import android.content.Context
     5→import android.content.Intent
     6→import android.util.Log
     7→import dagger.hilt.android.qualifiers.ApplicationContext
     8→import kotlinx.coroutines.CoroutineScope
     9→import kotlinx.coroutines.Dispatchers
    10→import kotlinx.coroutines.SupervisorJob
    11→import kotlinx.coroutines.flow.MutableSharedFlow
    12→import kotlinx.coroutines.flow.SharedFlow
    13→import kotlinx.coroutines.flow.asSharedFlow
    14→import kotlinx.coroutines.launch
    15→import javax.inject.Inject
    16→import javax.inject.Singleton
    17→
    18→/**
    19→ * Singleton manager for the Newland MT90 barcode scanner.
    20→ *
    21→ * Handles lifecycle management of the scanner BroadcastReceiver, processes scan events,
    22→ * implements debouncing to prevent duplicate scans, and provides a reactive SharedFlow
    23→ * for consuming scan results in ViewModels and UI layers.
    24→ *
    25→ * Key features:
    26→ * - Registers/unregisters BroadcastReceiver for nlscan.action.SCANNER_RESULT
    27→ * - Handles hardware scan button (F6 key)
    28→ * - Supports programmatic scan triggering via nlscan.action.SCANNER_TRIG
    29→ * - 300ms debounce protection against double-scans
    30→ * - Reactive API via SharedFlow<ScanResult>
    31→ *
    32→ * @property context Application context injected by Hilt
    33→ */
    34→@Singleton
    35→class NewlandScannerManager @Inject constructor(
    36→    @ApplicationContext private val context: Context
    37→) {
    38→    companion object {
    39→        private const val TAG = "NewlandScannerManager"
    40→
    41→        // Newland MT90 scanner broadcast actions
    42→        private const val ACTION_SCANNER_RESULT = "nlscan.action.SCANNER_RESULT"
    43→
    44→        // Intent extra keys for scan data (based on Newland MT90 SDK)
    45→        private const val EXTRA_BARCODE_DATA = "SCAN_BARCODE1"
    46→        private const val EXTRA_BARCODE_TYPE = "SCAN_BARCODE_TYPE"
    47→
    48→        // Debounce protection: ignore scans within 300ms of the previous scan
    49→        private const val DEBOUNCE_DELAY_MS = 300L
    50→    }
    51→
    52→    /**
    53→     * Tracks whether the BroadcastReceiver is currently registered.
    54→     * Prevents double registration/unregistration.
    55→     */
    56→    private var isRegistered = false
    57→
    58→    /**
    59→     * Timestamp of the last processed scan in milliseconds (System.currentTimeMillis()).
    60→     * Used for debounce protection to prevent duplicate scans within 300ms.
    61→     */
    62→    private var lastScanTimestamp = 0L
    63→
    64→    /**
    65→     * CoroutineScope for emitting scan events to SharedFlow.
    66→     * Uses SupervisorJob to isolate failures and Dispatchers.Main for UI thread safety.
    67→     */
    68→    private val scannerScope = CoroutineScope(SupervisorJob() + Dispatchers.Main.immediate)
    69→
    70→    /**
    71→     * Internal MutableSharedFlow for emitting scan results.
    72→     * Configured with replay = 0 (no replay) and extraBufferCapacity = 1 to buffer
    73→     * one event if no collectors are actively listening.
    74→     */
    75→    private val _scanEvents = MutableSharedFlow<ScanResult>(
    76→        replay = 0,
    77→        extraBufferCapacity = 1
    78→    )
    79→
    80→    /**
    81→     * Public SharedFlow for consuming scan results in ViewModels and UI layers.
    82→     *
    83→     * Collectors will receive ScanResult events emitted when the scanner captures
    84→     * a barcode. Use this in ViewModels with viewModelScope.launch { scanEvents.collect {...} }
    85→     *
    86→     * Example usage:
    87→     * ```
    88→     * viewModelScope.launch {
    89→     *     scannerManager.scanEvents.collect { scanResult ->
    90→     *         Log.d("MyViewModel", "Scanned: ${scanResult.barcode}")
    91→     *     }
    92→     * }
    93→     * ```
    94→     */
    95→    val scanEvents: SharedFlow<ScanResult> = _scanEvents.asSharedFlow()
    96→
    97→    /**
    98→     * BroadcastReceiver for handling scan results from the Newland MT90 scanner.
    99→     *
   100→     * Listens for nlscan.action.SCANNER_RESULT broadcasts and extracts barcode data
   101→     * and barcode type from the intent extras.
   102→     */
   103→    private val scanReceiver = object : BroadcastReceiver() {
   104→        override fun onReceive(context: Context?, intent: Intent?) {
   105→            if (intent?.action != ACTION_SCANNER_RESULT) {
   106→                return
   107→            }
   108→
   109→            val barcode = intent.getStringExtra(EXTRA_BARCODE_DATA)
   110→            val barcodeType = intent.getStringExtra(EXTRA_BARCODE_TYPE)
   111→
   112→            if (barcode.isNullOrBlank()) {
   113→                Log.w(TAG, "Received scan result with empty barcode")
   114→                return
   115→            }
   116→
   117→            val scanResult = ScanResult(
   118→                barcode = barcode,
   119→                barcodeType = barcodeType ?: "UNKNOWN"
   120→            )
   121→
   122→            Log.d(TAG, "Scan received: ${scanResult.barcode} (${scanResult.barcodeType})")
   123→
   124→            // Debounce protection: ignore scans within 300ms of the previous scan
   125→            val currentTime = System.currentTimeMillis()
   126→            val timeSinceLastScan = currentTime - lastScanTimestamp
   127→
   128→            if (timeSinceLastScan < DEBOUNCE_DELAY_MS) {
   129→                Log.d(TAG, "Scan ignored (debounce): ${timeSinceLastScan}ms since last scan")
   130→                return
   131→            }
   132→
   133→            // Update timestamp before emitting to prevent race conditions
   134→            lastScanTimestamp = currentTime
   135→
   136→            // Emit scan result to SharedFlow for reactive consumption
   137→            scannerScope.launch {
   138→                _scanEvents.emit(scanResult)
   139→                Log.d(TAG, "Scan event emitted to SharedFlow")
   140→            }
   141→        }
   142→    }
   143→
   144→    /**
   145→     * Registers the BroadcastReceiver to listen for scanner broadcasts.
   146→     *
   147→     * This should be called when the Activity/Fragment enters the foreground
   148→     * (typically in onResume or onStart) to begin receiving scan events.
   149→     *
   150→     * Safe to call multiple times - will not double-register.
   151→     */
   152→    fun register() {
   153→        if (isRegistered) {
   154→            Log.d(TAG, "Scanner receiver already registered, skipping")
   155→            return
   156→        }
   157→
   158→        val filter = android.content.IntentFilter(ACTION_SCANNER_RESULT)
   159→        context.registerReceiver(scanReceiver, filter)
   160→        isRegistered = true
   161→        Log.d(TAG, "Scanner receiver registered")
   162→    }
   163→
   164→    /**
   165→     * Unregisters the BroadcastReceiver to stop listening for scanner broadcasts.
   166→     *
   167→     * This MUST be called when the Activity/Fragment is destroyed (typically in
   168→     * onPause or onStop) to prevent memory leaks.
   169→     *
   170→     * Safe to call multiple times - will not crash if already unregistered.
   171→     */
   172→    fun unregister() {
   173→        if (!isRegistered) {
   174→            Log.d(TAG, "Scanner receiver not registered, skipping unregister")
   175→            return
   176→        }
   177→
   178→        context.unregisterReceiver(scanReceiver)
   179→        isRegistered = false
   180→        Log.d(TAG, "Scanner receiver unregistered")
   181→    }
   182→}
   183→

Perfect! I've successfully implemented the hardware button handler for KEYCODE_F6. Here's what was done:

## ✅ Subtask Completed: Hardware Button Handler (KEYCODE_F6)

### Changes Made:

1. **Added Scanner Trigger Constant**
   - Added `ACTION_SCANNER_TRIG = "nlscan.action.SCANNER_TRIG"` constant for programmatic scan triggering

2. **Added KeyEvent Import**
   - Imported `android.view.KeyEvent` for hardware button handling

3. **Implemented `onKeyDown()` Method**
   - Handles KeyEvent.KEYCODE_F6 (hardware scan button on MT90)
   - Returns `true` if F6 key is pressed, `false` otherwise
   - Follows standard Android KeyEvent handling patterns
   - Includes comprehensive KDoc with usage example

4. **Implemented `triggerScan()` Helper Method**
   - Private method to send scan trigger broadcast
   - Sends Intent with ACTION_SCANNER_TRIG to Newland scanner service
   - Includes logging for debugging

### Usage Example:
Activities can now handle the hardware scan button by overriding `onKeyDown`:
```kotlin
override fun onKeyDown(keyCode: Int, event: KeyEvent): Boolean {
    if (scannerManager.onKeyDown(keyCode, event)) {
        return true
    }
    return super.onKeyDown(keyCode, event)
}
```

### Verification:
✅ Code compiles successfully (`./gradlew compileDebugKotlin`)  
✅ Committed to git with descriptive message  
✅ Implementation plan updated

   204→     * @return true if the key event was handled (F6 key pressed), false otherwise
   205→     */
   206→    fun onKeyDown(keyCode: Int, event: KeyEvent): Boolean {
   207→        if (keyCode == KeyEvent.KEYCODE_F6 && event.action == KeyEvent.ACTION_DOWN) {
   208→            Log.d(TAG, "Hardware scan button (F6) pressed")
   209→            triggerScan()
   210→            return true
   211→        }
   212→        return false
   213→    }
   214→
   215→    /**
   216→     * Internal method to trigger a scan by sending the nlscan.action.SCANNER_TRIG broadcast.
   217→     *
   218→     * This sends an intent to the Newland scanner service to initiate a barcode scan.
   219→     * The scan result will be received via the registered BroadcastReceiver listening
   220→     * for nlscan.action.SCANNER_RESULT.
   221→     */
   222→    private fun triggerScan() {
   223→        val intent = Intent(ACTION_SCANNER_TRIG)
   224→        context.sendBroadcast(intent)
   225→        Log.d(TAG, "Scan trigger broadcast sent")
   226→    }
   227→}
   228→

entries.detail:
1→# NewlandScannerManager with Lifecycle Management
     2→
     3→Implement the NewlandScannerManager as an Application-scoped singleton: register/unregister BroadcastReceiver for nlscan.action.SCANNER_RESULT, handle hardware button (KeyEvent.KEYCODE_F6), programmatic trigger via nlscan.action.SCANNER_TRIG, debounce(300ms) protection against double-scans, and scanEvents: SharedFlow<ScanResult> for reactive consumption by ViewModels.
     4→
     5→## Rationale
     6→The MT90's built-in barcode scanner is the primary input device — not the touchscreen. The existing POC registers a BroadcastReceiver but never unregisters it (memory leak). Competitors like Honeywell suffer from scanner software conflicts (pain-2-1) and Zebra's DataWedge requires complex middleware configuration (pain-1-2). Our direct broadcast integration is simpler and more reliable.
     7→
     8→## User Stories
     9→- As a warehouse worker, I want to press the hardware scan button and have the barcode instantly captured so that I can scan items without touching the screen
    10→- As a developer, I want a reactive scanner API (SharedFlow) so that any screen can observe scan results without tight coupling to the scanner lifecycle
    11→
    12→## Acceptance Criteria
    13→- [ ] NewlandScannerManager is singleton in Application scope (Hilt @Singleton)
    14→- [ ] BroadcastReceiver registers in register() and unregisters in unregister()
    15→- [ ] Hardware scan button (F6) triggers barcode scan
    16→- [ ] Programmatic trigger via trigger(timeoutSec) method works
    17→- [ ] scanEvents SharedFlow emits ScanResult(barcode, barcodeType) to collectors
    18→- [ ] 300ms debounce prevents duplicate scan events
    19→- [ ] No memory leak — receiver properly cleaned up on destroy
    20→- [ ] Validated on physical MT90 device (not emulator)
    21→
